content.text: L1/L2/L3 Support Model for Claude Applications

A tiered support model partitions customer issues by complexity and routes each tier to the team with the right skills and tools to resolve it. Without tiers, every issue lands in the same queue: senior engineers spend their time answering API key questions while customers wait hours for a response to a real model bug. The L1/L2/L3 model corrects this by creating distinct escalation paths, SLAs, and ownership responsibilities for each tier.

For Claude applications specifically, the tiered model must account for issue types that do not exist in classical software support: model quality reports, safety and policy incidents, prompt injection disclosures, and context window edge cases. These require different escalation paths and different specialist teams than infrastructure or authentication issues.

attrs.alt: Three support tiers side by side. L1 Frontline (green): handles 70-80% of tickets, first response under 4h, covers API keys, rate limits, prompt formatting, billing. L2 Technical (blue): 15-25% of tickets, first response under 2h, covers unexpected API behaviour, quality degradation, integration debugging. L3 Engineering Escalation (red): 3-7% of tickets, first response under 1h, covers confirmed model bugs, safety violations, data exposure, outage impact. Escalation arrows flow L1→L2→L3.

content.text: L1 handles the highest-volume, lowest-complexity issues. The L1 team is equipped with documented runbooks for every known issue category, access to the customer's account metadata (tier, usage, recent errors), and a playbook for the most common questions. An L1 engineer should be able to resolve 70–80% of inbound tickets without any escalation.

L1 issues are characterised by the fact that the resolution is in a runbook or FAQ. If the L1 engineer cannot find the resolution in the knowledge base within 30 minutes, the ticket escalates to L2 — not because the L1 engineer is wrong, but because the ticket requires more context or investigation than L1 can provide.

from dataclasses import dataclass
from enum import Enum
from datetime import datetime

class SupportTier(str, Enum):
    L1 = "L1"
    L2 = "L2"
    L3 = "L3"

class IssueCategory(str, Enum):
    AUTH_API_KEY          = "auth_api_key"
    RATE_LIMIT            = "rate_limit"
    PROMPT_GUIDANCE       = "prompt_guidance"
    BILLING               = "billing"
    UNEXPECTED_BEHAVIOUR  = "unexpected_behaviour"
    QUALITY_DEGRADATION   = "quality_degradation"
    INTEGRATION_BUG       = "integration_bug"
    SAFETY_VIOLATION      = "safety_violation"
    DATA_EXPOSURE         = "data_exposure"
    CONFIRMED_MODEL_BUG   = "confirmed_model_bug"

TIER_ROUTING: dict[IssueCategory, SupportTier] = {
    IssueCategory.AUTH_API_KEY:         SupportTier.L1,
    IssueCategory.RATE_LIMIT:           SupportTier.L1,
    IssueCategory.PROMPT_GUIDANCE:      SupportTier.L1,
    IssueCategory.BILLING:              SupportTier.L1,
    IssueCategory.UNEXPECTED_BEHAVIOUR: SupportTier.L2,
    IssueCategory.QUALITY_DEGRADATION:  SupportTier.L2,
    IssueCategory.INTEGRATION_BUG:      SupportTier.L2,
    IssueCategory.SAFETY_VIOLATION:     SupportTier.L3,
    IssueCategory.DATA_EXPOSURE:        SupportTier.L3,
    IssueCategory.CONFIRMED_MODEL_BUG:  SupportTier.L3,
}

SLA_FIRST_RESPONSE_HOURS: dict[SupportTier, float] = {
    SupportTier.L1: 4.0,
    SupportTier.L2: 2.0,
    SupportTier.L3: 1.0,
}

@dataclass
class SupportTicket:
    ticket_id: str
    category: IssueCategory
    customer_tier: str       # "free", "pro", "enterprise"
    opened_at: datetime
    assigned_tier: SupportTier | None = None

    def route(self) -> SupportTier:
        tier = TIER_ROUTING[self.category]
        # Enterprise customers: always L2 minimum
        if self.customer_tier == "enterprise" and tier == SupportTier.L1:
            tier = SupportTier.L2
        self.assigned_tier = tier
        return tier

    def sla_deadline(self) -> datetime:
        if self.assigned_tier is None:
            raise ValueError("ticket not yet routed")
        hours = SLA_FIRST_RESPONSE_HOURS[self.assigned_tier]
        return self.opened_at.replace(microsecond=0) + __import__("datetime").timedelta(hours=hours)

L2 handles issues that require investigation beyond the knowledge base: reproducing unexpected API behaviour, debugging a customer's integration, or assessing whether a quality report reflects a real model regression or a prompt design issue. L2 engineers have API access to replicate calls, access to internal error logs (with appropriate data handling), and a working understanding of the model's capabilities and limitations.

The most common L2 issue type for Claude applications is quality degradation reports: a customer reports that the model's outputs have changed, are worse than expected, or are inconsistent. L2 must determine whether this is a real model regression (escalate to L3), a prompt design issue (coach the customer), or an environmental factor (rate limiting, context window saturation, temperature setting).

L2 also owns the reproduction step for potential model bugs. Before escalating to L3 engineering, L2 must produce a minimal reproducible case: the exact prompt, exact API parameters, and the observed output alongside the expected output. An L3 engineer receiving a vague description — "the model is giving worse answers" — cannot diagnose a model bug. A minimal reproduction can.

L3 is not a support tier in the traditional sense — it is the handoff point where a customer issue enters the engineering incident process. When a ticket reaches L3, it means the issue is either a confirmed defect that requires an engineering fix, a safety or policy incident that requires the Policy team, or a data exposure that requires Legal and Privacy. The L2 engineer who escalates to L3 creates an incident ticket and transfers ownership to the on-call engineering team.
L2 acknowledges within 1h; timeline update every 4h
Acknowledge receipt; do not reveal investigation details
Legal determines communication; do not acknowledge until Legal clearance
Thank reporter; do not disclose investigation scope

Clear escalation criteria reduce the two most common failures: under-escalation (issues that should be L3 sitting at L2 for days) and over-escalation (L1 issues flooding L2 because the L1 team is unsure). Both failures have direct customer impact: under-escalation delays real bugs; over-escalation inflates L2/L3 queue depth and slows response to genuine escalations.

the resolution is not in the knowledge base after 30 minutes of investigation; the issue is reproducible and not a known error code; the customer is enterprise tier and has been waiting more than 2 hours without resolution.

a minimal reproduction has been produced and the behaviour cannot be explained by prompt design or API parameters; the issue matches a safety or policy concern; the customer reports potential data exposure; the same report has come from more than one independent customer (possible widespread regression).
the L1/L2 engineer has not yet checked the knowledge base; the customer has not provided a minimal reproduction (request it first); the issue is a documentation gap that should be fixed at L1 level.

Enterprise customers bypass the standard L1 queue and enter directly at L2, regardless of the apparent issue category. This is not because enterprise issues are more complex — many are identical to L1 issues — but because the enterprise SLA commits to faster response times and dedicated Technical Account Manager involvement. An enterprise customer who waits 4 hours for an API key reset is experiencing a support failure even if the issue itself is trivial.

Enterprise tickets that turn out to be L1-category issues are resolved by the L2 engineer without demotion — the customer receives L2 service regardless of the issue type. The TAM is notified of all enterprise tickets and owns the customer relationship throughout the resolution.

The L1 knowledge base is a living document. Every time an L1 engineer escalates a ticket because they could not find the answer in the knowledge base, they file a knowledge base gap. The L2 engineer who resolves the issue is responsible for writing the corresponding L1 runbook entry within 5 business days. This feedback loop closes the gap between what L1 can resolve and what actually arrives in the queue.
Fewer than 5% of L1 tickets should result in a knowledge base gap filing. A rate above 10% indicates the knowledge base is not keeping pace with API changes or new model capabilities.

Every new model version release triggers a knowledge base review. The L2 team reviews the release notes and pre-emptively creates L1 runbook entries for the most common questions the new version is likely to generate.
When an API parameter, endpoint, or model is deprecated, the L1 knowledge base is updated before the deprecation takes effect — not after customers start filing tickets about it.

SLA compliance is not a passive metric — breaches must be caught in flight, not in retrospect. Each tier has a first-response SLA, but response alone is insufficient. Resolution time targets also apply, and a ticket that receives a first response within SLA but then sits idle for 18 hours is still a program failure. The enforcement model combines automated alerting with ownership accountability.

Automated breach detection uses the ticket's opened_at timestamp against the tier SLA. When a ticket crosses 80% of the SLA window without a first response, a soft-alert fires to the assignee's channel. At 100%, the ticket auto-reassigns to the backup agent and fires a hard alert to the support manager. Enterprise tickets use a dedicated alert path that also notifies the assigned account executive, because SLA breaches for enterprise customers carry contractual credit implications.

The tier model's effectiveness depends on accurate first-touch classification. Misrouted tickets create two failure modes: L3 gets flooded with issues an L1 agent could have resolved using documentation, consuming engineering time that should go to genuine bugs. And L1 gets assigned model-safety or data-exposure tickets that require L3 authority but sit in the wrong queue while the clock runs. Both failure modes damage customer trust and make SLA compliance impossible to sustain.

Misrouting is almost never deliberate — it comes from vague ticket titles, templates that obscure the real issue category, or customers who describe symptoms rather than causes. The classification layer must not rely on customer-supplied labels alone. A classifier that reads the ticket body and applies the tier-routing table reduces misrouting rates by catching the signal in the text even when the customer-chosen category is wrong.

When the classifier detects a conflict between the customer-supplied category and the inferred tier, the ticket is flagged for manual review before assignment. This adds a small delay to those tickets but prevents systematic under-escalation of safety and data-exposure issues. The flag rate is a program health metric — a high flag rate indicates customers are finding the ticket form confusing and the form needs revision.
Common Failure Modes at Each Tier
L1 resolving issues without documentation links:

L1 agents under time pressure sometimes provide a direct answer instead of linking the documentation page that explains the underlying concept. This resolves the immediate ticket but produces repeat contacts on the same topic. Resolution metrics look good; self-service metrics stay flat. L1 quality review should audit whether closed tickets include a documentation link.
L2 escalating without a reproduction case:

L3 engineering agents can only debug model-layer issues if they can reproduce them. An L2 escalation that says "customer reports model quality degraded" without a prompt, model ID, temperature setting, and example output sends L3 into a guessing exercise. L2 escalation forms must require a reproduction case for model-behavior issues; the form should hard-block submission if the fields are empty.
L3 fixing issues in individual customer accounts:

L3 engineers are sometimes tempted to make per-account configuration changes to resolve an escalation quickly. This creates undocumented state, bypasses code review, and often means the fix is never generalised. L3's role is to diagnose, document, and hand off a fix to the owning product team — not to apply one-off patches to production.
All tiers treating workarounds as resolutions:

A workaround that restores customer function does not close the underlying issue. The ticket should be closed from the customer's perspective (they are unblocked) but an internal follow-up issue must be created in the engineering backlog. Without this handoff, the workaround becomes permanent by default and the root cause is never fixed.

The L1/L2/L3 model routes issues by complexity: L1 handles 70–80% of volume (auth, rate limits, prompt guidance, billing) with a 4-hour first-response SLA; L2 handles technical investigation (quality reports, integration debugging, reproducible edge cases) with a 2-hour SLA; L3 is the engineering handoff point for confirmed model bugs, safety and policy incidents, data exposure, and prompt injection disclosures. Enterprise customers enter at L2 minimum. Escalation requires a minimal reproduction for model-related L2→L3 escalations. The knowledge base is maintained by L2 engineers filling gaps identified through L1 escalations, with pre-emptive updates before every model version release.
